evidence_text: SWE-Bench Pro contains 1,865 problems drawn from 41 actively maintained repositories.
source_excerpt: Abstract: “SWE-Bench Pro contains 1,865 problems sourced from a diverse set of 41 actively maintained repositories.”

evidence_text: The benchmark is partitioned into 11 public repositories, 12 held-out repositories, and 18 proprietary commercial repositories.
source_excerpt: Abstract: “The benchmark is partitioned into a public set with open access to problems sourced from 11 repositories, a held-out set of 12 repositories and a commercial set of 18 proprietary repositories.”
note: This describes repository partitioning, not instance counts.
caveat: This describes repository partitioning, not instance counts.

evidence_text: The public subset contains 731 released instances.
source_excerpt: Section 3.3: “Public. We release 731 instances openly on HuggingFace and report the relevant statistics and model performances in this paper.”
note: This is the published public-set instance count.
caveat: This is the published public-set instance count.

evidence_text: The commercial subset contains 276 problems and is kept private while results are reported publicly.
source_excerpt: Section 3.3: “Commercial. For the commercial set of 276 problems sourced from startup repositories, we keep it private but report results publicly in this paper and will update in the leaderboard.”
note: This is the published commercial-set instance count and access policy.
caveat: This is the published commercial-set instance count and access policy.

evidence_text: The held-out subset contains 858 problems and is kept private for future overfitting checks.
source_excerpt: Section 3.3: “Held-Out. We hold out a set of 858 problems mirroring the public set but use a separate set of repositories. We keep this set private to test for overfitting in the future.”
note: This is the published held-out-set instance count and intended use.
caveat: This is the published held-out-set instance count and intended use.

evidence_text: SWE-Bench Pro tasks are described as long-horizon tasks that may take professional software engineers hours to days.
source_excerpt: Abstract: “Our benchmark features long-horizon tasks that may require hours to days for a professional software engineer to complete.”
note: This is a task-duration characterization stated by the paper.
caveat: This is a task-duration characterization stated by the paper.

evidence_text: Reference solutions average 107.4 lines of code across 4.1 files.
source_excerpt: Introduction: “On average, the reference solutions span 107.4 lines of code across 4.1 files.”
note: This quantifies typical patch size in the benchmark.
caveat: This quantifies typical patch size in the benchmark.

evidence_text: Every SWE-Bench Pro problem requires at least 10 lines of change, and more than 100 tasks require over 100 lines of modification.
source_excerpt: Introduction: “Every problem involves at least 10 lines of change, and over 100 tasks demand more than 100 lines of modification.”
note: This states minimum and upper-tail edit sizes.
caveat: This states minimum and upper-tail edit sizes.

evidence_text: Each task is evaluated in a containerized, language-specific environment, and the environments are to be released as pre-built Docker images.
source_excerpt: Section 3.2: “Each task is evaluated in a containerized, language-specific environment with full dependency resolution... All environments will be released as pre-built docker images to ensure that they are fully reproducible.”
note: This describes the evaluation environment setup.
caveat: This describes the evaluation environment setup.

evidence_text: The benchmark runs each test set three times and filters out tests that do not pass consistently to reduce dynamic or occasional test failures.
source_excerpt: Section 3.2: “We notice that some tests can be dynamic or fail occasionally. To mitigate it, we run each set of tests 3 times and filter out any test that doesn’t pass consistently.”
note: This evidence matters because it documents instability in raw tests and the filtering step used to bound flakiness.
caveat: This evidence matters because it documents instability in raw tests and the filtering step used to bound flakiness.

evidence_text: On the public set, GPT-5 achieved the highest reported resolve rate at 23.3%.
source_excerpt: Table 1: “OpenAI GPT-5 23.3” and Abstract: “GPT-5 achieving the highest score to date at 23.3%.”
note: This is the top reported public-set Pass@1 result in the paper.
caveat: This is the top reported public-set Pass@1 result in the paper.

evidence_text: On the commercial set, Claude Opus 4.1 achieved the highest reported resolve rate at 17.8%.
note: This is the top reported commercial-set resolve rate in the paper.
caveat: This is the top reported commercial-set resolve rate in the paper.

evidence_text: The best reported commercial-set score is lower than the best reported public-set score, indicating a public-to-commercial performance gap in this benchmark.
source_excerpt: Introduction: “We additionally observe a marked performance gap between the public and commercial sets” and “≤ 23.3% on the public set; ≤ 17.8% on the commercial set.”
note: The paper presents this as evidence that commercial enterprise codebases are more difficult.
caveat: The paper presents this as evidence that commercial enterprise codebases are more difficult.

evidence_text: For Claude Opus 4.1 failures, wrong solutions accounted for 35.9% and syntax errors for 24.2%.
source_excerpt: Section 6.2: “Opus 4.1 primarily fails on semantic understanding, with wrong solutions accounting for 35.9% of failures and syntax errors at 24.2%.”
note: This is part of the paper’s failure-bucket analysis.
caveat: This is part of the paper’s failure-bucket analysis.

evidence_text: For Claude Sonnet 4 failures, context overflow accounted for 35.6% and endless file reading for 17.0%.
source_excerpt: Section 6.2: “Sonnet 4 has context overflow as its primary failure mode (35.6%) and substantial endless file reading behaviors (17.0%).”
note: This is another model-specific failure distribution reported by the paper.
caveat: This is another model-specific failure distribution reported by the paper.

evidence_text: The paper states that language coverage is limited and that Java, C++, and Rust are underrepresented.
source_excerpt: Abstract: “SWE-Bench Pro contains 1,865 problems sourced from a diverse set of 41 actively maintained repositories.”
